subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for June 29, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasoun
sentences: Story 1 is about Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis..
This is a systematic review / meta-analysis from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients, with six diagnostic, 22 prognostic studies, and four studies that reported on both diagnostic and prognostic outcomes, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
A total of 32 studies were included, consisting of 3142 patients, with six diagnostic, 22 prognostic studies, and four studies that reported on both diagnostic and prognostic outcomes.
Studies suggest moderate to good diagnostic accuracy for detecting increased central venous and right atrial pressures (sensitivity 78-95%, specificity 80-90%).

Prognostic studies in cardiac patients found an association between VExUS greater than or equal to 2 and acute kidney injury (AKI; odds ratio [OR], 4.44; 95% CI, 2.34-8.43; moderate certainty) and mortality (OR, 3.17; 95% CI, 1.30-7.75, low certainty).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: This is mainly diagnostic/prognostic support; use it to frame fluid-tolerance assessment, not as a standalone trigger for diuresis or ultrafiltration.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Impact of higher versus lower PEEP on mortality in mechanicall
sentences: Story 2 is about Impact of higher versus lower PEEP on mortality in mechanically ventilated patients with Sepsis - A multicenter, multi-cohort observational analysis..
This is a cohort study from Journal of critical care.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients requiring mechanical ventilation across three cohorts (one multicentric prospective = Hyspec ICU, one monocentric prospective = SepDataNet, one monocentric retrospective = RetroS, how should we interpret Impact of higher compared with lower PEEP on mortality in mechanically ventilated patients with Sepsis - A multicenter, multi-cohort observational analysis?
In two cohorts, higher PEEP was associated with significantly lower mortality (RetroSep: 39.4% vs.
50.4% for ICU-mortality, p = 0.008; Hyspec-ICU: 22% vs.
41.7% for 30-day mortality, p = 0.029).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Useful for risk stratification and hypothesis generation, but avoid causal bedside changes unless supported by interventional evidence.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Incidence and Outcomes of Refractory Septic Shock per Consensu
sentences: Story 3 is about Incidence and Outcomes of Refractory Septic Shock per Consensus Clinical Criteria: A Multicohort Retrospective Study..
This is a cohort study from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients with septic shock, how should we interpret refractory septic shock and 2) to assess outcomes of patients with septic shock meeting refractory septic shock criteria compared with those who did not. Multicohort, retrospective study. For aim 1, a descriptive epidemiologic study was designed to ascert?
Of 35,914 patients assessed, 15,732 patients with septic shock were included.
Refractory septic shock occurred in 3423 patients (21.8%), with a cumulative incidence of 218 cases (95% CI, 211-224) per 1000 patients with septic shock.
Hospital mortality with refractory septic shock was 64.4%, and risk-adjusted hospital mortality odds were 4.87-fold higher (95% CI, 4.46-5.31) compared with patients without refractory septic shock.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as background evidence; look for confounding and whether the exposure is modifiable at the bedside.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.summary: A total of 32 studies were included, consisting of 3142 patients, with six diagnostic, 22 prognostic studies, and four studies that reported on both diagnostic and prognostic outcomes. Studies suggest moderate to good diagnostic accuracy for detecting increased central venous and right atrial pressures (sensitivity 78-95%, specificity 80-90%). Prognostic studies in cardiac patients found an association between VExUS greater than or equal to 2 and acute kidney injury (AKI; odds ratio [OR], 4.44; 95% CI, 2.34-8.43; moderate certainty) and mortality (OR, 3.17; 95% CI, 1.30-7.75, low certainty). For critically ill patients, no associations between VExUS greater than or equal to 2 and AKI (OR, 1.45; 95% CI, 0.70-2.99; very low certainty) and mortality (OR, 1.25; 95% CI, 0.77-2.03; low certainty) were found. The venous excess ultrasound (VExUS) grading system has gained recognition in acute and critical care medicine as a promising approach to evaluate venous congestion and optimize fluid management.
articles.analysis: This is mainly diagnostic/prognostic support; use it to frame fluid-tolerance assessment, not as a standalone trigger for diuresis or ultrafiltration.

pico.population: patients, with six diagnostic, 22 prognostic studies, and four studies that reported on both diagnostic and prognostic outcomes
pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: Main outcomes are those reported in the abstract; prioritize patient-centered outcomes such as mortality, ventilation duration, ICU stay, and adverse events.

keyResults: A total of 32 studies were included, consisting of 3142 patients, with six diagnostic, 22 prognostic studies, and four studies that reported on both diagnostic and prognostic outcomes.
Studies suggest moderate to good diagnostic accuracy for detecting increased central venous and right atrial pressures (sensitivity 78-95%, specificity 80-90%).

Prognostic studies in cardiac patients found an association between VExUS greater than or equal to 2 and acute kidney injury (AKI; odds ratio [OR], 4.44; 95% CI, 2.34-8.43; moderate certainty) and mortality (OR, 3.17; 95% CI, 1.30-7.75, low certainty).

For critically ill patients, no associations between VExUS greater than or equal to 2 and AKI (OR, 1.45; 95% CI, 0.70-2.99; very low certainty) and mortality (OR, 1.25; 95% CI, 0.77-2.03; low certainty) were found.
criticalAppraisal: High-yield synthesis: check heterogeneity, included study quality, and whether ICU patients match your setting.
Look for heterogeneity, small-study effects, and whether pooled outcomes are clinically comparable.
articles.practiceImpact: This is mainly diagnostic/prognostic support; use it to frame fluid-tolerance assessment, not as a standalone trigger for diuresis or ultrafiltration.

articles.title: Incidence and Outcomes of Refractory Septic Shock per Consensus Clinical Criteria: A Multicohort Retrospective Study.
articles.summary: Of 35,914 patients assessed, 15,732 patients with septic shock were included. Refractory septic shock occurred in 3423 patients (21.8%), with a cumulative incidence of 218 cases (95% CI, 211-224) per 1000 patients with septic shock. Hospital mortality with refractory septic shock was 64.4%, and risk-adjusted hospital mortality odds were 4.87-fold higher (95% CI, 4.46-5.31) compared with patients without refractory septic shock. Consensus clinical criteria for refractory septic shock were recently developed, but the incidence and outcomes with these criteria are unknown. There were two aims: 1) to describe the incidence of refractory septic shock and 2) to assess outcomes of patients with septic shock meeting refractory septic shock criteria compared with those who did not.
articles.analysis: Use as background evidence; look for confounding and whether the exposure is modifiable at the bedside.

pico.intervention: refractory septic shock and 2) to assess outcomes of patients with septic shock meeting refractory septic shock criteria
pico.comparator: those who did not. Multicohort, retrospective study. For aim 1, a descriptive epidemiologic study was designed to ascert
pico.outcomes: Clinical criteria for refractory septic shock were operationalized as concomitant occurrence of norepinephrine (base) equivalent greater than 0.5 µg/kg/min and lactate greater than 2 mmol/L. Risk-adjusted hospital mortality was assessed with a generalized linear mixed-effects model with a random effect for hospital and fixed effects for age, sex,...

keyResults: Of 35,914 patients assessed, 15,732 patients with septic shock were included.
Refractory septic shock occurred in 3423 patients (21.8%), with a cumulative incidence of 218 cases (95% CI, 211-224) per 1000 patients with septic shock.
Hospital mortality with refractory septic shock was 64.4%, and risk-adjusted hospital mortality odds were 4.87-fold higher (95% CI, 4.46-5.31) compared with patients without refractory septic shock.
Clinical criteria for refractory septic shock were operationalized as concomitant occurrence of norepinephrine (base) equivalent greater than 0.5 µg/kg/min and lactate greater than 2 mmol/L.
criticalAppraisal: Observational evidence: useful for association and prognosis, but residual confounding is likely.
Check confounding by indication, adjustment variables, immortal-time bias, and missing outcome data.
articles.practiceImpact: Use as background evidence; look for confounding and whether the exposure is modifiable at the bedside.

I would interpret this cautiously because Check confounding by indication, adjustment variables, immortal-time bias, and missing outcome data..
